findings.title: loadSkillFromDir is async but uses only synchronous I/O
findings.description: The function is declared async and returns Promise<Skill>, but its body only uses fs.readFileSync (synchronous). There is no await or asynchronous operation inside the function. This creates an unnecessary promise wrapper and misleads callers into thinking the function performs async I/O. Similarly, discoverSkills (line 96) is async but only calls synchronous fs methods plus loadSkillFromDir (which itself is sync in practice).

findings.title: activate() and deactivate() are near-identical methods
findings.description: The activate() and deactivate() methods are structurally identical -- the only difference is the boolean value assigned to entry.active. This is 10 lines of near-duplicate code. While the codebase prefers clarity over premature abstraction (and three similar lines are acceptable), these two methods could share a private helper to reduce the duplication while maintaining clear public API names.

findings.title: Three identical Array.isArray checks could use a loop
findings.description: Three consecutive blocks each check Array.isArray on an optional field and assign it to metadata. The pattern is identical -- only the field name varies. Per the project convention of 'three similar lines > premature abstraction', the current form is acceptable for clarity. However, if more optional array fields are added in the future, a loop over field names would be cleaner.

findings.title: Registry test recreates identical config object in every test case
findings.description: The same SkillRegistry instantiation with identical config appears 12 times across the test file. Each test case repeats the 3-line constructor call. While test explicitness is valued, a helper function or shared setup would reduce noise without sacrificing clarity.

positive_observations: All source functions are short (<45 lines) with low cyclomatic complexity (max CC=5 in parseSkillMd), well within project thresholds
Consistent use of early returns and guard clauses keeps nesting depth at 2 or below in all source files
Type definitions are clean and well-documented with JSDoc comments explaining each interface's purpose
The context-integration module is a textbook example of small pure functions composed together (extractLayer1 -> formatLayer1Summary -> buildSkillsContext)
No nested ternaries, no magic numbers, no deep nesting -- the code follows established codebase conventions
